title: Geometric Instability and Self-Limitation in Driven Quantum Systems
abstract: We develop a unified geometric framework for local non-adiabaticity in driven quantum systems. We show that the previously introduced AMT non adiabaticity parameter arises as a special realization of a more general geometric instability criterion governed by the normalized Fubini Study distinguishability speed. The local geometric evolution speed is identified as the physically relevant quantity controlling the onset of non-adiabatic instability.   We introduce a universal dimensionless instability parameter measuring the competition between quantum-state evolution speed and spectral-gap protection. This quantity provides a local, gauge-invariant, and basis-independent criterion for arbitrary driven Hamiltonians. Near quantum critical points, the instability parameter diverges through inverse gap amplification, recovering the Kibble Zurek freeze-out condition directly from local geometric data.   We prove that monotonic occupation-dependent nonlinear regulators geometrically compress the quantum metric, establishing a self-limitation theorem in which nonlinear spectral deformation confines the accessible region of projective Hilbert space under strong driving. The multimode extension yields a matrix-valued instability criterion that identifies collective instability channels invisible to scalar descriptions.   The framework naturally extends to open quantum systems through the Bures metric and quantum Fisher geometry, where thermal mixing and Lindblad decay increase the instability threshold through geometric suppression of state distinguishability. The instability threshold further implies a universal geometric lower bound on coherent control time and quantum gate duration.
summary: This paper establishes a universal geometric framework using the normalized Fubini–Study distinguishability speed to quantify non-adiabatic instability in driven quantum systems. It introduces a dimensionless parameter ($\mathcal{A}_n$) that predicts instability onset, connects this to quantum critical phenomena, and proves a self-limitation theorem for nonlinear systems. The theory offers a unified diagnostic tool applicable from superconducting qubits to magnonic resonators.
main_problem: The paper develops a unified geometric framework to quantify local non-adiabaticity and instability in general driven quantum systems, moving beyond traditional adiabatic theorems.
main_result: It introduces a universal, gauge-invariant instability parameter ($\mathcal{A}_n$) that governs the onset of non-adiabaticity, and proves a 'Geometric Self-Limitation' theorem for nonlinear systems.
method: The framework utilizes the Fubini–Study metric and the Quantum Geometric Tensor (QGT) to define the instability parameter, which is then analyzed in both closed and open quantum system settings.
model_or_system: The theory applies to general driven quantum systems, exemplified by parametric oscillators, coupled bosonic modes, and is extended to open systems using the Bures metric.
key_observables: Universal instability parameter ($\mathcal{A}_n$), Fubini–Study speed ($v_{FS}$), spectral gap ($\Delta_n$), and the maximum eigenvalue of the instability matrix ($\lambda_{\max}$).
important_parameters: The instability threshold ($\mathcal{A}_n \sim 1$), the minimum gate time ($T_{min} = 1/\sqrt{8\omega}$), and the nonlinear regulator strength ($U$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the assumption that the accessible low-occupancy manifold is compact and that the nonlinear regulator provides a monotonic gap condition for its self-limitation proof.
figures_summary: The notes reference calculations confirming the relationship between the FS metric and the geometric parameter ($\eta^2\Omega^2/8$), and show how the dominant eigenvalue ($\lambda_{\max}$) increases with inter-mode coupling ($J$).
paper_structure: The paper builds from defining the general geometric instability criterion ($\mathcal{A}_n$) using the FS speed and spectral gap, demonstrating its equivalence to older parameters, and then extending this to analyze self-limitation via nonlinear regulators and multi-mode coupling.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful, unified geometric diagnostic tool ($\mathcal{A}_n$) applicable across diverse quantum platforms (qubits, magnonics) to predict fundamental limits on control speed and stability.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
